ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class; no specific pork-filled steamed bun entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers steamed bread/bun category; no pork-filled variant code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steamed pork bun. Null used to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard steamed pork bun weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a snack or light meal item.

nutrient_source.title: 鲜肉包 Steamed Pork Buns — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 鲜肉包 Steamed Pork Buns (cr_id G00010071). The HPB record reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which is inconsistent with the known composition of wheat-flour bun dough (typically 40–50 g/100g). This anomaly is documented in notes. All other values are preserved as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun (baozi) — analogous GI estimate based on steamed white bread / wheat-flour steamed products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 鲜肉包 Steamed Pork Buns in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: steamed white wheat-flour bun / baozi (GI ~65, medium category), which reflects the refined wheat flour wrapper component. The pork filling contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect GI. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100. Because the HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g — which is a known data anomaly — the GL is recorded as 0.0 pending correction of the carbohydrate value. If the actual carbohydrate content (~40 g/100g) were used, GL per 80 g serving would be approximately 20.8 (high). This anomaly is documented in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit steamed pork buns; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is 1.75× the upper safe threshold, and soy sauce and oyster sauce in the filling are the primary contributors — request reduced-sauce preparation or substitute with a lower-sodium filling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour bun wrapper is high-GI (estimated GI ~65) with significant carbohydrate load (~40 g/100g actual); HPB-reported 0 g carbohydrate is a known data anomaly
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit steamed pork buns due to the refined wheat flour wrapper, which contributes a high glycaemic load; portion size should be restricted to one small bun and paired with low-GI foods, and wholemeal flour variants should be requested where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), moderate fat (10.5 g/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) from pork filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit steamed pork buns due to the combination of high sodium, moderate saturated fat from pork, and dietary cholesterol at 89 mg/100g, all of which contribute to elevated cardiovascular risk; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) and pork-derived arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit steamed pork buns; pork filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and the high sodium load further exacerbates systemic inflammation — consider chicken or tofu filling alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Pork Buns (鲜肉包, Xiān Ròu Bāo) are a classic Chinese dim sum and street food item widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. Each bun consists of a soft, fluffy, leavened wheat-flour dough wrapper that is filled with a seasoned fresh pork mixture — typically minced pork shoulder or belly seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, ginger, and spring onion — then steamed until the wrapper is pillowy and the filling is fully cooked. The texture is characteristically soft and slightly chewy on the outside with a juicy, savoury, umami-rich filling. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore database reports 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 1050 mg sodium. The carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g reported by HPB is a known data anomaly; the actual carbohydrate content from the wheat flour wrapper is typically 40–50 g/100g. Clinically, steamed pork buns are high-concern for hypertension (sodium 1050 mg/100g), Type 2 Diabetes (high-GI refined wheat flour wrapper, estimated GI ~65), and cardiovascular disease (sodium, fat, and cholesterol combination). They are not halal due to pork content. In Singapore, they are commonly sold at hawker centres, dim sum restaurants, and Chinese bakeries as a breakfast, lunch, or snack item.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a steamed pork bun?
Can diabetics eat steamed pork buns?
Is 鲜肉包 high in sodium?
What is the GI of baozi?
Is pau babi suitable for hypertension?
How much protein in a steamed pork bun?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010071), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The carbohydrate anomaly (0.0 g/100g) is documented and does not reduce confidence in the source — it reflects a known HPB data entry issue for this record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: CARBOHYDRATE ANOMALY: The HPB source records carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g/100g and sugar_g as 0.0 g/100g for this steamed pork bun. This is inconsistent with the known composition of wheat-flour bun dough, which typically contributes 40–50 g carbohydrate per 100 g of finished product. The 0.0 g value is preserved as reported by the approved source (HPB) but is flagged as a likely data entry error. Downstream users should treat carbohydrate_g = 0.0 as suspect and cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central entry for steamed pork bun / baozi before clinical use. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is recorded as 0.0 per the HPB approved source; this value is not naturally zero — it is a suspected data entry anomaly in the HPB record, not a confirmed zero. Sugar_g is recorded as 0.0 per HPB; this is plausible given the savoury filling but is also suspect given the carbohydrate anomaly. Dietary_fibre_g is recorded as 0.0 per HPB; this is plausible for refined wheat flour with minimal vegetable content. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB steamed pork bun record are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GLYCEMIC LOAD NOTE: GL per serving is recorded as 0.0 because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g (data anomaly). If actual carbohydrate (~40 g/100g) is used, GL per 80 g serving = (65 × 32) / 100 = 20.8 (high). HALAL STATUS: This product contains pork and is not halal.
